questions.question: What is the specificity score of the selector `.nav-list .nav-link:hover`?
options: 3 points (three elements)
21 points (two classes + one pseudo-class)
30 points (three classes/pseudo-classes)
12 points (one class + one element + one pseudo-class)
questions.explanation: Class selectors and pseudo-classes each count as 10 points. `.nav-list` (10) + `.nav-link` (10) + `:hover` (10) = 30 points. Pseudo-classes like :hover are scored the same as class selectors.

questions.question: In the CSS box model, what is the correct order from innermost to outermost?
options: Margin → Border → Padding → Content
Content → Padding → Border → Margin
Content → Border → Padding → Margin
Padding → Content → Border → Margin
questions.explanation: The box model layers from inside to outside are: Content (the actual text/image), Padding (space inside the box around the content), Border (the visible edge), and Margin (space outside the box pushing other elements away).

questions.question: What does `box-sizing: border-box` do?
options: It removes the border from the element's total width
It makes the width and height include padding and border, not just content
It adds a box-shaped border around the element
It sets the element's display to block
questions.explanation: With `box-sizing: border-box`, the `width` and `height` you set include padding and border inside that measurement. Without it (the default `content-box`), padding and border are added on top of the width, making elements wider than expected.

questions.question: What is the difference between `display: inline` and `display: inline-block`?
options: There is no difference — they behave the same way
Inline elements can have width and height set; inline-block elements cannot
Inline-block elements can have width, height, and vertical margins; inline elements cannot
Inline-block elements always start on a new line; inline elements do not
questions.explanation: Inline elements ignore width, height, and top/bottom margins. Inline-block elements flow horizontally like inline elements, but they respect width, height, and all margins — giving you the best of both worlds.

questions.question: Which CSS color format allows you to set a semi-transparent background without affecting the text inside?
options: The opacity property
rgba() or hsla()
Named colors with the transparent keyword
questions.explanation: `rgba()` and `hsla()` let you set transparency on just the color itself (e.g., `background-color: rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.5)`). The `opacity` property, by contrast, makes the entire element and all its children transparent — including text.

questions.question: Which CSS properties are GPU-accelerated and best for smooth animations?
options: width and height
margin and padding
transform and opacity
top, left, right, and bottom
questions.explanation: `transform` and `opacity` are GPU-accelerated, meaning the browser can animate them on a separate layer without recalculating the layout of the page. Properties like width, height, margin, and top cause layout recalculations (reflows) that are more expensive and can cause janky animations.

questions.question: Where should the `transition` property be placed for a hover effect?
options: On the :hover pseudo-class
On the base state of the element (not the :hover)
On the parent element
It doesn't matter — either location works identically
questions.explanation: The `transition` property should be placed on the base state (e.g., `.btn`), not on the `:hover` state. This ensures the transition animates both when the user hovers (entering) AND when they move away (leaving). Placing it on :hover would only animate the entrance, and the exit would snap back instantly.

questions.question: What is the purpose of CSS custom properties (variables) defined on `:root`?
options: They only apply to the <html> element itself
They create JavaScript variables accessible from CSS
They define global values that can be referenced anywhere in the stylesheet with var()
They override all other styles with the highest specificity
questions.explanation: CSS custom properties defined on `:root` (which targets the `<html>` element) are available globally throughout the stylesheet. Any rule can reference them with `var(--property-name)`. This makes it easy to maintain consistent colors, fonts, spacing, and other values from a single location.

questions.question: Two sibling paragraphs have `margin-bottom: 20px` and `margin-top: 16px` respectively. How much space appears between them?
options: 36px (the margins add together)
20px (the larger margin wins due to margin collapse)
16px (the smaller margin wins)
18px (the browser averages the two values)
questions.explanation: This is margin collapse in action. When top and bottom margins of adjacent block elements meet, they don't add up — the larger margin wins. The 20px bottom margin and 16px top margin collapse into a single 20px gap.

questions.question: In BEM naming convention, what does the class `.card--featured` represent?
options: An element inside the card component called 'featured'
A modifier that represents a variation of the card component
A child block nested inside the card block
A pseudo-class applied to the card on hover
questions.explanation: In BEM (Block, Element, Modifier), double hyphens (`--`) indicate a modifier — a variation of the block or element. `.card--featured` is a modified version of the `.card` block. Elements use double underscores (`.card__title`), and modifiers use double hyphens (`.card--featured`).
